data.title: ClearBridge International Value Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equity markets posted positive returns in the second quarter, with a series of bilateral U.S. trade deals and prospect of greater government spending in Europe overcoming tariff concerns, fears of economic slowing and geopolitical tensions. As a result, the core MSCI (MSCI) EAFE Index rose 11.8%, outperforming the 10.9% return of the S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index. Strong performance in the more cyclical communication services and information technology (IT) sectors helped growth stocks reclaim market leadership with the MSCI EAFE Growth Index (13.5%) outperforming the MSCI EAFE Value Index (10.1%).

  • Contributors: Holdings in Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY) Energy, SK Hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF) and Shinhan Financial (SHG). Stock selection in the industrials, consumer discretionary and materials sectors. Stock selection in emerging markets, Europe Ex U.K. and Japan.

  • Detractors: Holdings in Willis Towers Watson (WTW), Sanofi (SNY), Gerresheimer (OTCPK:GRRMF). Stock selection in the health care sector, underweight to communication services and overweight to energy. Overweight to emerging markets and the U.K., stock selection in the U.K.

  • Outlook: The world has certainly given investors much to ponder over the coming month. Although we have some educated guesses, we simply don't know what the future holds. All we can do is lean on our investment philosophy and process to prepare for various outcomes.

Top Equity Issuers (% of Total)

Holding Fund
Siemens AG (OTCPK:SIEGY) 3.37
Fujitsu Ltd (OTCPK:FJTSF) 3.22
Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSF) 2.94
AXA SA (OTCQX:AXAHY) 2.70
Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG) Inc 2.58
Lloyds Banking Group ((LLDTF) (LYG)) PLC 2.53
Hitachi Ltd (OTCPK:HTHIY) 2.46
Shell PLC (SHEL) 2.33
Sanofi SA (SNY) 2.30
SAP SE (SAP) 2.23

Performance Review

  • The Fund outperformed its benchmark in the second quarter, boosted by strong stock selection within the industrials and financial sectors, as well as regional selection within emerging markets and Europe.

  • Our high-quality industrials companies rebounded from a soft first quarter and continue to see strong demand for products and services related to the long-term trends of electrification, the energy transition and infrastructure.

  • One of our top contributors was Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SMEGF), a German power generation and transmission equipment company that continues to demonstrate strong operational and improving financial performance, bolstered by increased electrification investments globally.

  • Our financials holdings continued their strong performance, driven by banks such as Austria's BAWAG (OTCPK:BWAGF) and U.K.-based Lloyds (LYG). Large European banks have been major beneficiaries of increasing liquidity as capital is reallocated from the U.S. onto the Continent.

  • South Korean companies Shinhan and SK Hynix contributed to outperformance in emerging markets. The companies rallied following the South Korean election, as uncertainty gave way to optimism as the new government is viewed as business and market friendly.

  • Health care proved an obstacle to relative performance, largely driven by stock-specific headwinds to our holdings Sanofi. A French pharmaceutical company, Sanofi came under pressure after it revealed disappointing trial data on a phase III trial for a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) treatment amid a broader strategic push by the company to advance its internal research and development efforts over the past few years.

  • Also in health care, Gerresheimer, a German manufacturer of packaging solutions for the health care and cosmetic industries, continues to struggle to gain ground amid an industry-wide backlog of inventory due to overstocking during the COVID-19 pandemic.

  • Stock selection in the U.K. detracted due to concerns that a slowing macroeconomic environment, sticky inflation and a lack of government stability would weigh on investor sentiment.

  • We initiated a position in Valterra Platinum (OTCPK:ANGPY), a South African precious metals miner specializing in platinum group minerals, which was recently demerged from mining giant Anglo American (OTCQX:AAUKF). Valterra is poised to be a major beneficiary of the increasing demand for platinum due to its use in a variety of applications in electronics, infrastructure and other uses at a significantly higher margin profile than its peers.

  • We exited our position in New Oriental Education (EDU) & Technology, a for-profit education and tutoring company in China, due to several quarters of poor performance that spurred questions about the company's long-term growth trajectory.

  • We also exited French electrical company Schneider Electric (SBGYS, SBGSF) — which has performed very strongly both fundamentally and in terms of share price — as we believe that the company has reached a fair valuation.

Sector Allocation (Equity as a % of Total)

Sector Fund
Financials 23.81
Industrials 21.85
Information Technology 11.85
Consumer Discretionary 9.81
Materials 8.25
Health Care 6.83
Consumer Staples 6.32
Energy 4.09
Utilities 2.70
Communication Services 2.62
Real Estate 1.14

Outlook

  • The world has certainly given investors much to ponder over the coming months: When, and to what extent, will the Federal Reserve cut rates? What will be the effect of tariffs on inflation and global economic growth? Will European countries enact further defense and infrastructure spending? Will the Chinese government enact further measures should growth continue to be anemic? Although we have some educated guesses, we simply don't know what the future holds. All we can do is lean on our investment philosophy and process to prepare for various outcomes.

  • Uncertainty, however, often leads to opportunity. As value investors, an uncertain future allows us to venture beyond the boundary of what is known and comfortable to find high-quality companies trading at a large enough discount to more than compensate for the risk. Uncertain demand and competitive dynamics have begun to shake the market's confidence in previous market darlings in industries such as luxury goods, food and beverages, and health care. The rapid change in the trajectory of the U.S. dollar has begun to raise concerns surrounding the earnings of companies that have been significant beneficiaries of the dollar's previous strength. We believe these concerns will present us with attractive opportunities in the future.

ClearBridge International Value Fund Average annual total returns and fund expenses (%) - as of June 30, 2025

data.title: July Jobs Report Stuns Wall Street, Fed Rate Cut In Play, Key Charts
data.description:
The July jobs report came in much weaker than expected. Just 73,000 positions were added, below the +110,000 consensus, but the real stunner was the sharp negative revisions to May and June payrolls. The previous two months were taken down by 258,000, the largest shaving since May 2020.

The unemployment rate climbed to the highest level since October 2021, at 4.248%. So, it was a whisker from rounding up to 4.3%. Average hourly earnings rose by 0.3%, right on with the Wall Street target, while the year-on-year worker pay growth rate is now 3.9%, which was ahead of estimates. A bright spot was a 0.1-hour jump in the average weekly hours worked, helping to offset the light headline payrolls addition.

Private payrolls were also soft at just 83,000; June’s figure was revised lower to just 3,000. Government and manufacturing employment contracted in July, while the U-6 underemployment rate rose from 7.7% to 7.9%.

There was a steep decline in the number of foreign-born workers--that's a key story since lower immigration numbers will bring down the break-even pace of job growth needed to keep the unemployment rate steady. It may now be in the 70k-90k range.

After the dust settled, the odds of a September rate cut jumped from 40% to 69%, and the chorus of easing from Fed officials could grow louder and expand from just Governors Waller and Bowman.

In terms of market price action, the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) plummeted to under 3.8% in the minutes after the 8:30 a.m. ET data, down more than 15 basis points. Stocks actually barely budged, but the US Dollar Index (DXY) plunged 1.6%, with the EURUSD (EUR:USD) currency pair climbing from $1.14 to $1.156. Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) spiked 1.8%, and bitcoin (BTC-USD) was steady. The S&P VIX Index (VIX) neared 19.

A Weak July Jobs Report

Unemployment Rate Climbs to the Highest Rate Since October 2021, 3-Month Average Payroll Growth Slowest Since June 2020

Jobless Rate Rises 0.13%

Private Payrolls Also Soft

All the Employment Gain Came from Education & Health Services

Two-Month Revisions: Worst Since 2020

Household Survey Negative

69% Chance of A Sept Cut, Two Quarter-Point Eases Priced Into 2025

Recession Risk Rises from 10% to 13%

X Poll: Fintwit Was Close

Stocks Little Changed After the NFP, -1%

2-Year Yield Plunges

Dollar Drops Hard After A Steep Rally

President Trump May Be Right

Looking ahead, a light data week is on tap after the onslaught of macro volatility catalysts in recent days. Factory Orders and the Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey on Bank Lending (SLOOS) hit Monday.

June trade data, a final look at S&P Global PMIs, and July ISM Services come Tuesday. Claims and productivity figures, along with the NY Fed’s Consumer Inflation Survey, arrive on Thursday—that's also when President Trump’s reciprocal tariffs are set to take effect.

Key Data On Tap

The Bottom Line

After mainly solid jobs reports in 2025, the July employment situation was much weaker than expected. The headline payrolls climb fell far short of economists’ estimates, and revisions to May and June were dreadful. This report pressures Chair Powell and the Fed to consider resuming rate cuts in September.

data.title: Clarivate: Upside Is Attractive If Execution Holds
data.description:
Investment action

I gave a buy rating for Clarivate (NYSE:CLVT) previously, as I thought the business was going to see organic growth inflect upwards, and should momentum keep up, the stock should re-rate higher. With 2Q25 marking the second straight quarter of organic growth and further acceleration in ACV and recurring revenue, I believe the thesis is playing out. The underlying momentum across all three segments, combined with clear adoption traction for CLVT’s AI tools, reinforces my view that this turnaround is real. At ~5.7x NTM PE, the stock still looks cheap, and I continue to see material upside if execution stays on track.

2Q25 Earnings Review

CLVT's 2Q25 performance added further credibility to the turnaround narrative. While revenue of $621.4 million did fall by 4.4% y/y, this shouldn’t be alarming since it was entirely driven by planned portfolio rationalization. The more important figure is organic revenue, which grew 0.5% y/y, marking the second straight quarter of positive growth and an acceleration from the 0.3% seen in 1Q25. This improvement was also fairly broad-based. Academia & Government [A&G] stood out with 2.5% organic growth, while Life Sciences & Healthcare [LS&H] returned to growth at 0.3%. Even though the IP segment declined 2.1% organically, there were signs of recovery in its core patent renewals business. Profitability was also impressive. Adj. EBITDA hit $261.6 million, translating to a strong 42.1% margin, and adj. EPS came in at $0.18.

Organic Growth Inflection Is Forming A Credible Trend

After looking at the 2Q25 performance, I believe we can now say this organic growth inflection is becoming a credible trend. More importantly, forward-looking indicators are looking very healthy. Organic Annualized Contract Value [ACV], which I take as the best measure of momentum in the core subscription business, accelerated to 1.3% growth. This is a 40 bps improvement from FY24 and also shows that adoption is gradually picking up. The quality of this growth is also important to note, and the metric to monitor here is recurring organic revenue, which strips out the more volatile transactional business. This metric accelerated to 0.8% growth in the quarter (up from 0.6% y/y in 1Q25). Put together, I think it really shows that CLVT’s Value Creation Plan is successfully re-engineering the business model toward higher-quality, more predictable revenue. And if this becomes a sustainable trend over the coming quarters, CLVT should earn a higher valuation multiple from the market.

Underlying Business Health Is Stronger Than Ever

Now, if we dig further into other operating metrics, the turnaround story looks even more credible. One of the clearest signs is the improvement in the annual renewal rate, which ticked up to 93% in 1H25 from 92% vs. last year. While that may not seem like a lot, it is actually very meaningful in a subscription-based model because it reflects (1) the value proposition continues to resonate, (2) CLVT sales execution remains healthy, and (3) customer lifetime value [CLTV] goes up, which enhances CLVT’s long-term profit runway.

The other impressive thing is the transformation in CLVT’s revenue mix. As CLVT continues to pivot away from lower-quality transactional revenue, CLVT organic recurring revenue mix is now at 88%, up 800 bps vs. last year and a 500 bps step up from 1Q25. I believe this has further de-risked the business fundamentally, and it is logical because higher recurring revenue brings better visibility and more stable cash flow.

Finally, it was also very good to see that the breadth of the recovery was supported by all three core segments, validating that the turnaround is not just a one-off but the result of sound execution across the board. For reference, A&G saw 2% organic ACV and subscription revenue growth in 1H25, despite budget pressures in its end markets. Importantly, 75% of A&G’s FY25 subscription base had already been renewed by July, and this provides strong revenue visibility going into 2H25. In the IP segment, the rebound in patent renewals (which I believe is the most important recurring revenue stream) is notable. Organic growth of ~1.5% in 1H25 signals stabilization, and I think this is an early inning of further growth acceleration as CLVT is well positioned to benefit from the structural increase in patent filings being driven by the AI boom. On the other hand, LS&H investments are clearly starting to pay off. The return to organic ACV growth in 1H25, driven by improved retention in the Cortellis suite, suggests that customer demand has improved, and if that holds, subscription revenue should follow.

AI Strategy Is Delivering Tangible Results and a Competitive Edge

In my May write-up, I discussed that CLVT’s AI strategy is no longer just talk. I have even more conviction this is true after looking at the progress made in 2Q25. As of 2Q25, more than 4,800 institutions are actively using CLVT’s AI-powered tools, up from more than 3,000 in 1Q25, clearly suggesting that there is a strong product-market fit. The fact that research firm Outsell named Clarivate as the “industry leader” in developing agentic AI tools for scholarly workflows also shows that product-market fit is solid.

In my view, the most important aspect here is how AI is being embedded into core platforms like Web of Science, especially in features with high utilization like the literature review agent. As I said before, these tools solve real pain points by saving researchers time and improving accuracy, which ultimately boosts the overall value proposition of the CLVT platform. I also mentioned that this would drive higher customer stickiness and lower churn, but one additional area I am convinced CLVT can benefit from is that this also opens the door for upselling premium AI tiers. As more users understand the value and restructure their workflow to leverage AI, CLVT could roll out AI tiers (with better features, etc.) to meaningfully expand ARPU.

Valuation

When I said CLVT was cheap at 6x NTM PE, I didn’t expect it to get cheaper (now at 5.7x) despite the improvements in fundamentals. I believe the market has not fully digested the improvement, as the headline revenue decline did not screen well. Like I have said above, organic growth continues to inflect, underlying operating metrics are getting healthier, and CLVT’s AI strategy is shaping up to be a real growth driver. All of these should warrant multiples to go higher, and at this valuation, even a modest upgrade to the -1 standard deviation level of 7.8x NTM PE would yield amazing upside. I remind readers that Dun & Bradstreet Holdings (DNB) trades at a premium to CLVT with similar revenue growth rates, so that is a precedent that the market is willing to attach a higher multiple.

Risk

Despite the encouraging progress, CLVT turnaround is still in its early phase. Organic growth, while improving, remains low and heavily reliant on execution of its Value Creation Plan. External risks are also notable, particularly the threat of university and government funding cuts, which could pressure A&G demand. Lastly, the ongoing strategic review introduces uncertainty, with no resolution expected until early 2026. While the direction of travel is positive, these risks mean the investment case still carries execution and macro risk.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for CLVT. The business is clearly turning a corner, with organic growth now stabilizing and key forward indicators trending in the right direction. The revenue mix is shifting toward higher-quality recurring streams, and AI adoption is progressing well. With improving fundamentals and a cheap valuation, I see a compelling upside profile. Execution remains the key, but if CLVT delivers, I believe the stock is well-positioned for a meaningful re-rating.

data.title: S&P500: Euphoria And Bubble Face The New World Reality
data.description:
The context

The S&P500 (SP500) is entering August trading at a bubble-like valuation second only to the peak of the 2000 dot-com bubble, based on the Shiller PE ratio.

Consistent with the high valuation, the sentiment is exuberant based on many measures, which points to euphoria.

At the same time, the global economy is entering a new protectionist state with the increase in tariffs on August 1st, which is widely expected to cause an increase in inflation and slower growth over the next two quarters, with significant stagflationary implications.

Thus, the bubble in the S&P500 is likely to burst as the euphoria fizzles with the first obvious signs of stagflation, which is already creeping up in data, but currently ignored by the market participants.

Signs of Euphoria

There are many measures of market sentiments, and they all point to the increased signs of euphoria in the market.

The Citi's Levkovich Index for example, measures margin levels, option pricing, and short interest, and it's showing the return on euphoria. The Index's threshold for euphoria is 0.38, and it hit 0.65 last Friday. In fact, this is the third highest level of euphoria, behind the 2000 dot.com bubble and 2021 post-pandemic retail-led meme stock mania.

Barclay's Equity Euphoria Indicator EEI measures the speculative flows in derivatives, futures, and options. Similarly to the Levkovich Index, the EEI is also indicating euphoria in the market just like at the peak of the 2000 dot.com bubble, and the post-pandemic meme stock mania.

The bubble

The S&P500 is trading at the bubble-like valuations, above the post-pandemic meme stock mania, and just below the 2000 dot-come bubble valuation, based on the Shiller PE ratio, which is currently just below 39.

Thus, the measures of euphoria match the bubble valuations.

Note, the 2025 forward ratio for the S&P500 is near 25. The bubble is led by the tech mega-caps, associated with the AI theme.

The argument is that these tech companies are producing significant profits, which was not the case in 2000 with the dot-coms. Thus, the current situation is not comparable with 2000. However, the fact is that those earnings are overpriced by the market, by a large amount, and that's reflected in the PE ratio. The PE ratio simply normalizes the price with earnings, and these tech earnings are overpriced. The Information Technology ETF (XLK) is trading at a 2025 forward ratio of 33, so these expected 2025 earnings are very expensive.

But it is not just the tech that's overpriced. Costco (COST) is trading at a PE ratio of 53. WalMart's (WMT) PE is 38. Starbucks (SBUX) has a forward PE ratio of 41. Thus, the bubble is much broader. These are all large consumer-oriented companies that should not be trading at these valuations, given their growth prospects.

Note, Consumer Staples ETF (XLP) is trading at a PE ratio of 22, and this is supposed to be a defensive sector, a value play. Given the valuation, even the staples are expensive.

The new world reality

The Trump administration has been imposing tariffs on the US trading partners gradually since February.

First, in February, the Trump administration imposed introductory tariffs on China, in addition to Mexico and Canada. Then on April 2nd, the Trump administration imposed the 10% universal tariff, with some sector-based tariffs, and additional country-based tariffs. Finally, on August 1st, the US imposed the reciprocal tariffs, with an increase in the universal tariff to 15% for countries that have trade deficits with the US.

The average total US tariff now is somewhere between 15% and 20%, probably closer to 20% - and that's very high compared to virtually no tariffs last year.

In addition, tariffs are expected to keep increasing. There are still some sector-based tariffs that are expected to be imposed, like semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. The trade deal with China is still being negotiated, and it's likely that tariffs on China could increase. Specifically, the 50% secondary tariffs on countries trading with Russia are expected to be imposed "soon", which could affect China and India.

The fact is that the free trade regime has been replaced with protectionism - and this is expected to boost inflation and slow growth. The tariffs seem to be connected with the escalating geopolitical situation, based on the tariffs on Brazil, and the secondary tariffs. That's the new word reality.

Implications

The market participants have been ignoring the "new world reality" since the selloff in response to the April 2nd tariffs. There are three reasons for this:

First, the market participants believed that Trump would negotiate trade deals by the August 1st deadline and that the universal tariff would remain at 10% - there would be no increase in tariffs. This thesis has now been proven wrong - tariffs substantially increased on August 1st, and will like to keep climbing.

Market participants were encouraged by the fact that the labor market has remained solid, and inflation has not spiked in response to the tariffs. Thus, the expectation was that the economy would remain resilient, and the Fed would be able to cut interest rates two times in 2025. The Fed has stated that it will continue to be data-dependent, which rules out the automatic interest rate cuts in 2025. Inflation is showing an early reaction to tariffs, with rising good prices, while the labor market is softening. Growth is slowing significantly, after adjusting for volatile net exports. More importantly, growth is expected to slow in Q3 after the front-loading of consumption ahead of tariffs in Q2.

The third reason is the AI trade. The AI capex is still strong and increasing, however, once the economy slows even the tech mega-caps will not be immune, given their exposure to consumer spending.

Over the next two quarters, inflation is likely to keep rising, while growth is likely to keep slowing, and it is very likely we could have an inflationary recession. The first confirming sign of deteriorating data is likely to bust the bubble in the S&P500 as the euphoria fizzles - and likely gets replaced with panic.

data.title: Rocket Lab Stock Surges With Potential Golden Dome Win (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB) stock has surged nearly 75% since my June report significantly outperforming the S&P 500’s 7% gain. With the stock now trading well above my $34.22 price target, it is a good time to reassess the valuation and revisit catalysts for the stock price. In this report, I introduce an additional valuation framework to support my continued Buy rating.

Rocket Lab Shows Rapid Response Capability

Rocket Lab stock started its strong market outperformance in mid-June. From financial perspective, there were no noteworthy items that would warrant the stock price to run up as much as it did. I reviewed recent news items and could also not detect any major event. There, however, was one announcement that I consider to be a big positive. The company did announce two launches in 2025 for a confidential commercial customer. While not necessarily huge news, the announcement marked Rocket Lab’s ability to quickly go from contract to launch with the first launch scheduled just four days after the announcement. The second launch is also expected before the end of the year.

The company further signaled demand for more than 20 launches in 2025. I believe that the quick turn also provides support to an improving launch cadence. Launch schedules remain fluid, so adding two launches does not necessarily mean that the company will definitely launch two additional missions this year, but it possibly could which would also positively feather into the financial performance of the company.

Golden Dome Opportunity Could Be Huge

Rocket Lab has increasingly focused on improving its launch capabilities and its space systems capabilities. The development of the Neutron rocket allows the company to launch payloads in a different payload class while its expanding space systems portfolio positions the company more and more as a vertically integrated launch provider and a one-stop-shop solution. To that, we can add that Rocket Lab is increasingly focusing on providing solutions in the interest of national security and is also receiving contracts to compete for launch services adding further credibility to the company’s capabilities and position in the market.

I recently marked Rocket Lab as one of the companies that could benefit from the Golden Dome missile defense systems. I believe that Lockheed Martin (LMT) could be the lead integrator on the program. Rocket Lab could play a major role in terms of providing launch services and space systems engineering. Especially with the quarrel between President Trump and Elon Musk, I believe that Rocket Lab is a bit better positioned than Musk’s SpaceX to support the space-based parts of the missile defense program. That is not because SpaceX is not a capable launch provider, but because President Trump rather does not deal with companies or CEOs with whom he is not aligned and given that Musk threatened to decommission the Dragon spacecraft as part of his fallout with President Trump, it may not seem to be in the best national security interest to award SpaceX an exclusive contract and perhaps under the current conditions it may not be wise to include SpaceX at all.

That leads to a somewhat more prominent position for Rocket Lab to provide launch services and justifies an upbeat sentiment. At the same time, we do note that a lot is not known about the Golden Dome project. It is an ambitious project, but how it will be structured is not known. If space-based interceptors will be used, there is a huge opportunity for the likes of Rocket Lab but that is not a certainty as the budget and timeline in my view remain challenging.

Layering on top of the Golden Dome opportunity are opportunities in Europe as Europe is seeking space and security autonomy. Recently, Rocket Lab was selected to launch a pair of satellites for the LEO-PNT future navigation constellation.

Assessing The Price Target For Rocket Lab

Rocket Lab stock received a bullish coverage from Citi with a price target hike from $33 per share to $50 per share based on a more mature business in 2029. While I do not necessarily disagree with that, I do believe that basing the stock price target in 2025 on the expected results in 2029 is ambitious and perhaps only a suitable target for those who are extremely convinced of the company’s success in the long run and the patience required to get to that point.

I am not saying that the $50 target, which already has been reached in July, is not realistic but I do believe we should look at what stock prices are fundamentally supported in the nearer future. To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Normally, I use a price-to-sales method to provide preliminary assessment on the price target for companies that are not profitable yet. Rocket Lab is not expected to be profitable on EBITDA basis until 2026. Running a peer group valuation provides a $1.86 price target in 2026 and $9.47 for 2027. If we allow the company’s stock price to be driven by an EV/EBITDA multiple that is twice that of the peer group, we get a $18.90 price target. The lack of profits in the near term and the ramp in profits thereafter is clearly the reason why an EV/EBITDA valuation implies significant downside. As I believe that an enterprise value method better captures the capital structure of a company than a simple price-to-earnings or price-to-sales method for maturing companies, I have also considered an EV-to-sales valuation.

An EV/Sales valuation shows that in the years ahead, there is no tangible upside to the stock price. However, we do see support for the $50 price target that Citi analysts provided. In fact, our price target calculation exceeds $55 per share. At the same time, we do note that this only provides 21% upside from current levels and while I remain bullish on the long-term prospects for Rocket Lab, meaning post-2030, I do not believe that the strong buy rating can be maintained with only 21% upside by 2029. In other words: As bright as the prospects are for Rocket Lab, the stock price has run up a bit too much for a strong buy rating to be maintained and I am downgrading the stock to buy. Below a stock price of $41.22 and definitely below $28.68, a strong buy rating could be reapplied.

High Stock Price Leaves Little Room For Neutron Rocket Development Delays

While Rocket Lab is enjoying a lot of positive momentum with a growing national security market including Golden Dome opportunities, I do believe that risks such as the development of the Neutron rocket and the certification and associated timeline should also be considered and the current stock price provides extremely little padding for that. I am not marking the stock a sell, since there is no indication that Neutron rocket development is running behind on schedule but if it did there is a major risk in the current share price due to the recent surge.

Conclusion: Rocket Lab Has Upside For The Future

I definitely do like Rocket Lab as a company. That was the case years ago when I covered Rocket Lab even before it was public company and it is the case now. However, I also do recognize that the current stock price values the company years ahead of sales and earnings which invites risk. Investors have to be aware of these risks and weight the current valuation against the risks and timeline. I am downgrading the stock from strong buy to buy, noting both the long-term opportunities and the near-term schedule risks that exist with any launch vehicle development.
